steps.title: Resolve the parasite paradox
steps.content: The passage sets up an expectation that three-host CLPs might be protected because they are transmitted when an infected host is eaten, shielding them from external conditions. Yet Wood et al. found that three-host CLP abundance decreased as sea temperatures rose, while directly transmitted parasite abundance stayed largely stable.

steps.title: Reject contradicted explanations
steps.content: A says more host species means better protection from warming, but the three-host parasites declined. C compares subtypes of CLPs that the passage does not distinguish. D reverses the passage by saying directly transmitted parasites were more shielded than three-host CLPs. The only supported explanation is that the shielding advantage was real but insufficient against other warming-related pressures. The answer is B.
